claim: Ni-free LDS microstructure consisted of the γ-matrix with fine κ-carbides.
evidence_text: Ni-free LDS microstructure consisted of the γ-matrix with fine κ-carbides

claim: The Ni-containing low Mn alloy possessed coarser κ-carbides along with B2 in both bamboo-like stringer and polygonal particle morphology in the γ-matrix.
evidence_text: Ni containing low Mn alloy possessed coarser κ-carbides along with B2 in both bamboo-like stringer and polygonal particle morphology in the γ-matrix.

claim: Hardness of the γ-matrix and B2 phase were similar, and B2 grains exhibited distinct pop-in events in nanoindentation curves indicating incipient plasticity.
evidence_text: Hardness of the γ-matrix and B2 phase were similar with B2 grains exhibiting distinct pop-in events in the nanoindentation curves indicating incipient plasticity.

claim: The γ-matrix and B2 co-deform near yield; a favourable orientation relationship between γ-matrix and B2 facilitated easy slip transfer, while non-favourable orientation relationship controlled ductility by strain accumulation and B2-cracking.
evidence_text: The γ-matrix and B2 co-deform near yield, and a favourable orientation relationship (OR) between the γ-matrix and B2 facilitated easy slip transfer, while the non-favourable OR controlled ductility by strain accumulation and B2-cracking.

claim: Austenite-based LDS alloys have four major microstructural features: γ-matrix, L12 ordered κ-carbide particles, B2 ordered Fe(Ni)Al intermetallic compounds, and DO3 ordered Fe3Al precipitates.
evidence_text: Austenite (γ) based LDS alloys have four major microstructural features: (i) the γ-matrix, (ii) L12 ordered (FeMn)3AlC (κ-carbide) particles, (iii) B2 ordered Fe(Ni)Al type intermetallic compounds, and (iv) DO3 ordered Fe3Al precipitates [7–9].

claim: Based on size, shape and site, B2 morphology can be classified as elongated stringers across γ-grains, intergranular spherical (200 nm – 1 μm) at recrystallised γ-grain boundaries, and intragranular precipitates (50 – 300 nm) within shear bands of deformed γ-grains.
evidence_text: Based on the size, shape, and site of occurrence, the morphology of B2 can be classified as (i) elongated cylindrical shapes called stringers which exist across several γ-grains, (ii) intergranular spherical (200 nm – 1 μm) at the recrystallised γ-grain boundaries, and (iii) intragranular precipitates (50 – 300 nm) formed within the shear bands in the deformed γ-grains [10].
